questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a mole?

choices.text: The mass in grams of one atom of an element
choices.explanation: One atom's mass in grams is called an atomic mass unit (amu), not a mole. A mole is $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles of any substance.
A mole is defined as exactly $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.). This allows conversion between mass and number of particles.

choices.text: The volume occupied by a gas at STP
choices.explanation: 22.4 L is the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP (273 K, 1 atm), not the definition of a mole. A mole is defined by the number of particles, not a volume.

choices.text: The number of neutrons in one gram of hydrogen
choices.explanation: This is a common misconception. One gram of hydrogen contains $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms (1 mole), but a mole is defined as Avogadro's number of particles, not neutrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A sample of iron has a mass of 55.85 g. The molar mass of iron is 55.85 g/mol. How many moles of iron are in the sample?

choices.explanation: 0.5 mol would require dividing 55.85 g by 111.7 g/mol, which is not the molar mass of iron.
$n = m/M = 55.85\ \text{g} / 55.85\ \text{g/mol} = 1.0\ \text{mol}$. The mass equals the molar mass, so there is exactly 1 mole.
55.85 mol would require a sample of 55.85 × 55.85 ≈ 3119 g of iron. Dividing mass by molar mass gives moles: 55.85/55.85 = 1.0 mol.
2.0 mol of iron would have mass 2 × 55.85 = 111.7 g. The sample is only 55.85 g, which equals exactly 1.0 mol.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The mass spectrum shown is for a pure element. What is the average atomic mass of this element?

choices.explanation: 63.0 u would be the average if only the lighter isotope existed (100% abundance). The weighted average must account for the 31% abundance of the m/z = 65 isotope.
64.0 u is the simple arithmetic average of 63 and 65. Weighted average requires multiplying each mass by its fractional abundance: $(0.69)(63) + (0.31)(65) = 43.47 + 20.15 = 63.62$ u.
Weighted average atomic mass = $(0.69)(63) + (0.31)(65) = 43.47 + 20.15 = 63.62$ u. This is consistent with copper (63.55 u) which has two naturally occurring isotopes.
65.0 u would be the average if only the heavier isotope existed. Since the lighter isotope (m/z = 63) has 69% abundance, it dominates and the average is closer to 63.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Two atoms of the same element have atomic numbers of 17 but mass numbers of 35 and 37. These atoms are best described as

choices.explanation: Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element (e.g., diamond and graphite). Atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers are isotopes.

Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same number of protons, so same atomic number) with different mass numbers (different number of neutrons). Both atoms are chlorine with 17 protons but different neutron counts.
Isobars are atoms of different elements with the same mass number. These atoms have different mass numbers (35 and 37) and the same atomic number — they are isotopes.
Ions are atoms that have gained or lost electrons. These neutral atoms differ only in neutron count, making them isotopes, not ions.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A compound is found to contain 40.0% carbon, 6.7% hydrogen, and 53.3% oxygen by mass. What is the empirical formula?

choices.explanation: $C_2H_4O_2$ has the same elemental ratio as $CH_2O$ but is not the simplest whole-number ratio. The empirical formula must be the simplest ratio: $CH_2O$.
Converting: C = 40/12 = 3.33, H = 6.7/1 = 6.7, O = 53.3/16 = 3.33. Dividing by 3.33: C:H:O = 1:2:1. The empirical formula is $CH_2O$, not $CHO$.
Moles: C = 40.0/12.0 = 3.33; H = 6.7/1.0 = 6.7; O = 53.3/16.0 = 3.33. Dividing by smallest (3.33): C:H:O = 1:2:1. Empirical formula = $CH_2O$.
$CH_3O$ would require H:C = 3:1. The calculation gives H:C = 6.7:3.33 ≈ 2:1, so the formula is $CH_2O$, not $CH_3O$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following electron configurations represents a ground-state atom in the third period?

choices.explanation: This configuration has 16 electrons total (2+2+6+2+4 = 16), corresponding to sulfur (S, Z=16), a Period 3 element. All subshells are filled in Aufbau order.
Period 3 elements fill up to 3p subshell. $3d$ orbitals belong to Period 4 (4th shell fills d orbitals). This configuration would belong to a transition metal in Period 4.
2p subshell can hold a maximum of 6 electrons, not 8. This is an invalid electron configuration.
This configuration has 19 electrons corresponding to potassium (K), which is a Period 4 element (its outermost electron is in n=4). This is not a Period 3 configuration.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Photoelectron Spectrum of Unknown Element X

questions.question: A photoelectron spectrum for element X shows three peaks with relative electron counts of 2 (highest binding energy), 8 (medium binding energy), and 1 (lowest binding energy). Which element is most consistent with this spectrum?

choices.explanation: Neon has 10 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$. Its PES would show peaks with counts of 2, 2, and 6 — not 2, 8, and 1.
Sodium has 11 electrons: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$. PES peaks: 2 electrons (highest BE, $1s^2$); 8 electrons (medium BE, $2s^2 + 2p^6$); 1 electron (lowest BE, $3s^1$). Matches 2, 8, 1.
Magnesium has configuration $[Ne]3s^2$. PES would show 2, 8, 2 — three peaks with the lowest-BE peak having 2 electrons, not 1.
Lithium has only 3 electrons: $1s^2 2s^1$. Its PES would show only two peaks (counts 2 and 1), not three peaks.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the elements Li, C, and Ne in order of increasing first ionization energy?

choices.explanation: This is the reverse of the correct order. Neon has the highest ionization energy in Period 2 due to its large nuclear charge and complete valence shell.

First ionization energy generally increases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge with similar shielding. Li (Z=3) < C (Z=6) < Ne (Z=10). Note: O is slightly less than N due to electron pairing, but Li < C < Ne holds.
Carbon (Z=6) has a higher ionization energy than lithium (Z=3) because of greater nuclear charge and higher effective nuclear charge across the period.
Neon (Z=10) has higher ionization energy than carbon (Z=6) because it has more protons with similar shielding, and its full valence shell is particularly stable.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Moving from left to right across Period 3, which of the following properties shows a DECREASING trend?

choices.explanation: Electronegativity increases across a period because nuclear charge increases while shielding stays approximately constant, increasing the attraction for bonding electrons.
First ionization energy generally increases across a period (with minor exceptions) because higher nuclear charge holds valence electrons more tightly.

Atomic radius decreases across a period. As nuclear charge increases with similar electron shielding, electrons are pulled closer to the nucleus, reducing the radius. Na has the largest radius; Ar has the smallest in Period 3.
Effective nuclear charge increases across a period as more protons are added without proportionally more shielding from core electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The table shows first and second ionization energies for four Period 3 elements. Which element shows the most dramatic jump between the first and second ionization energies, and what best explains this?

choices.text: Magnesium; it has an unusually stable configuration after losing 2 electrons
choices.explanation: Magnesium shows a jump (738 to 1451 kJ/mol) but not the most dramatic. Sodium shows the largest jump from 1st IE (496) to 2nd IE (4562) — a factor of ~9.

choices.text: Sodium; after losing its single valence electron, the 2nd electron must come from a filled core shell
choices.explanation: Na has one 3s valence electron (IE₁ = 496). The 2nd electron would come from the 2p core shell, which is much closer to the nucleus with far less shielding. This dramatic jump (496 → 4562) identifies Na as having one valence electron.

choices.text: Silicon; it has the highest 1st IE in the table
choices.explanation: Silicon has the highest 1st IE (786 kJ/mol) in this table, but the relative jump from 1st to 2nd IE is not the largest. Sodium's 10-fold jump is far more dramatic.

choices.text: Aluminum; removing the 2nd electron disrupts its $3s^2$ configuration
choices.explanation: After losing 1 electron, Al has $[Ne]3s^2$ configuration. The 2nd IE of Al (1817) is not dramatically higher than the 1st (577) compared to Na's jump. Sodium's jump is the most dramatic.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: An element has the electron configuration $[Ne]3s^2 3p^3$. How many valence electrons does it have, and what charge would its most common ion have?

choices.text: 3 valence electrons; most commonly forms a 3+ ion
choices.explanation: 5 electrons (not 3) occupy the n=3 shell: $3s^2 3p^3$. This is phosphorus (P). Phosphorus more commonly gains 3 electrons to achieve an octet (P³⁻), rather than losing 5.

choices.text: 5 valence electrons; most commonly forms a 3− ion
choices.explanation: Valence electrons are in n=3: $3s^2 + 3p^3 = 5$ electrons. Phosphorus (P) needs 3 more electrons to complete its octet, forming P³⁻ in ionic compounds.

choices.text: 5 valence electrons; most commonly forms a 5+ ion
choices.explanation: While P can form +5 oxidation states in covalent compounds, as an ionic species P more commonly gains 3 electrons (P³⁻) rather than losing all 5 valence electrons.
The total valence electrons in n=3 are $3s^2 + 3p^3 = 5$. Only counting 3s electrons (2) would miss the 3p electrons that are also valence electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the first ionization energy of oxygen (Z=8) is slightly lower than that of nitrogen (Z=7), despite oxygen having a higher nuclear charge?

choices.text: Oxygen has more electrons than nitrogen, increasing electron-electron repulsion overall
choices.explanation: While oxygen has more total electrons, the specific reason for the exception is about orbital occupancy in 2p, not general electron count.

choices.text: Nitrogen has a half-filled 2p subshell that is unusually stable due to exchange energy
choices.explanation: Nitrogen's $2p^3$ configuration places one electron in each 2p orbital (Hund's rule). This half-filled arrangement has extra stability from electron exchange interactions. In oxygen, two electrons must share a 2p orbital, experiencing extra repulsion — making the second electron in that orbital easier to remove.

choices.text: Oxygen's electrons are in higher-energy orbitals than nitrogen's
choices.explanation: Both N and O have valence electrons in 2p orbitals — the same subshell. The exception occurs because of electron pairing within a 2p orbital in oxygen, not because of different subshells.

choices.text: Oxygen atoms are larger than nitrogen atoms, so valence electrons are held less tightly
choices.explanation: Oxygen atoms are actually smaller than nitrogen atoms (atomic radius decreases across a period). The correct explanation involves electron pairing repulsion in oxygen's 2p subshell.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A sample of water ($H_2O$, molar mass 18 g/mol) has a mass of 36 g. How many molecules of water are in this sample?

choices.explanation: This would be 1 mole. 36 g / 18 g·mol⁻¹ = 2 moles. Two moles contains $2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules.
$n = 36/18 = 2\ \text{mol}$. Number of molecules = $2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.
This is $0.5 \times N_A$, which would correspond to 0.5 mol (9 g of water). The sample has 36 g = 2 mol = $1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.
2.0 is the number of moles, not molecules. To convert moles to molecules, multiply by Avogadro's number: $2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following electron configurations is NOT correct for a ground-state atom?

choices.explanation: This is the correct ground-state configuration for magnesium (Mg, Z=12). Electrons fill according to Aufbau principle.
This is the correct ground-state configuration for fluorine (F, Z=9).
This configuration skips filling the 3s subshell before 3p. The Aufbau principle requires 3s to be filled before 3p. The correct configuration for 11 electrons (Na) is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$.
This is the correct ground-state configuration for nitrogen (N, Z=7).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Using the data in the table, what is the average atomic mass of boron?

choices.explanation: 10.5 is the simple average of 10 and 11 without weighting by abundance. The weighted average must account for the 80.1% abundance of B-11.
Weighted average = $(0.199)(10.012) + (0.801)(11.009) = 1.992 + 8.818 = 10.81\ \text{u} \approx 10.8\ \text{u}$. The higher abundance of B-11 pulls the average closer to 11.
11.0 u would be correct only if B-11 had 100% abundance. With 19.9% B-10 contributing, the average falls below 11.
10.0 u would result if only B-10 were considered. Both isotopes contribute: $(0.199)(10.012) + (0.801)(11.009) \approx 10.8$ u.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Coulomb's law states that electrostatic force is proportional to $\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$. Which of the following best explains why effective nuclear charge increases across Period 2?

choices.text: The number of core electrons increases, reducing shielding
choices.explanation: Core electrons in Period 2 remain at 2 (the 1s² electrons). They do not increase across the period. The change is in nuclear charge, not core electrons.

choices.text: The nuclear charge increases while the number of core shielding electrons remains approximately constant
choices.explanation: Across Period 2, each element gains one proton (+1 to nuclear charge). The 1s² core electrons (2 electrons) stay the same throughout. With more protons but similar shielding, effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) increases across the period.

choices.text: The atomic radius increases, reducing the Coulombic attraction
choices.explanation: Atomic radius decreases across a period — it does not increase. The increasing effective nuclear charge pulls electrons closer, shrinking the atom.

choices.text: Valence electrons shield each other completely from the nucleus
choices.explanation: Valence electrons in the same shell shield each other poorly (compared to core electrons). If they completely shielded, effective nuclear charge would not increase across the period.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following changes results in formation of an ion with the same electron configuration as a noble gas?

choices.explanation: Ca has configuration $[Ar]4s^2$. Losing 2 electrons gives $Ca^{2+}$ with configuration $[Ar]$ — identical to argon, a noble gas configuration.

Carbon gaining 2 electrons gives $C^{2-}$ with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ — the same as neon. This IS a noble gas configuration. However, $C^{2-}$ is not a common stable ion in chemistry, whereas $Ca^{2+}$ is.

Nitrogen losing 3 electrons gives $N^{3+}$ with configuration $1s^2 2s^2$ (only 4 electrons) — this matches helium structure if we consider 2 electrons, but not a standard noble gas configuration. Nitrogen typically gains electrons, not loses them.
Oxygen losing 2 electrons gives $O^{2+}$ with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$ — this is a carbon-like configuration, not a noble gas.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A compound of carbon and hydrogen contains 85.7% C and 14.3% H by mass. The molar mass is determined to be 56 g/mol. What is the molecular formula?

choices.explanation: $CH_2$ is the empirical formula (molar mass = 14 g/mol). Since 56/14 = 4, the molecular formula is 4 times the empirical formula.
$C_2H_4$ has molar mass = 28 g/mol. Since the actual molar mass is 56 g/mol and 56/28 = 2, the molecular formula should be twice $C_2H_4 = C_4H_8$, not $C_2H_4$.
Empirical: C = 85.7/12 = 7.14; H = 14.3/1 = 14.3. Ratio: C:H = 7.14:14.3 = 1:2, so empirical formula = $CH_2$ (MW = 14). Ratio = 56/14 = 4. Molecular formula = $(CH_2)_4 = C_4H_8$.
$C_3H_6$ has molar mass = 42 g/mol. The actual molar mass is 56 g/mol. $C_3H_6$ does not match the given molar mass.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following subatomic particles primarily determines an atom's chemical properties?

choices.text: Neutrons, because they stabilize the nucleus
choices.explanation: Neutrons contribute to nuclear stability and mass number but do not significantly affect chemical behavior, which depends on electron configuration.

choices.text: Protons, because they determine the atomic number
choices.explanation: While protons determine atomic number (and thus the element), the chemical properties are directly determined by electron configuration, which is how atoms interact with other atoms.

choices.text: Electrons, because they are involved in bond formation and chemical reactions
choices.explanation: Chemical reactions involve making and breaking bonds, which requires electrons. The number and arrangement of electrons (especially valence electrons) determine how atoms interact, form bonds, and behave chemically.

choices.text: Quarks, because they make up protons and neutrons
choices.explanation: Quarks are subcomponents of protons and neutrons at the nuclear physics level. They do not participate in chemical reactions, which occur at the electron/bond level.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Going down Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) from Be to Ba, which of the following trends is correct?

choices.text: Atomic radius decreases; ionization energy increases
choices.explanation: Going down a group, atomic radius increases (more electron shells are added). Ionization energy decreases because the outer electrons are farther from the nucleus and better shielded.

choices.text: Atomic radius increases; ionization energy decreases
choices.explanation: Going down a group: each element adds a new electron shell (larger radius) and the outer electrons are farther from the nucleus with more shielding (lower ionization energy).

choices.text: Atomic radius increases; ionization energy increases
choices.explanation: Larger atoms with outer electrons farther from the nucleus and better shielded require less energy to ionize — ionization energy decreases going down a group.

choices.text: Atomic radius decreases; ionization energy decreases
choices.explanation: Atomic radius increases going down a group because each element has more electron shells. The premise of decreasing radius is incorrect.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A researcher measures the photoelectron spectrum of an unknown element. The spectrum shows four distinct peaks at binding energies of approximately 2800 eV, 130 eV, 20 eV, and 5 eV. The relative heights of the peaks correspond to 2, 2, 6, and 2 electrons respectively.

questions.question: Based on the photoelectron spectrum described, how many valence electrons does the unknown element have, and what is its most likely identity?

choices.text: 2 valence electrons; the element is beryllium (Be)
choices.explanation: Beryllium has only 4 electrons total with two peaks (2 and 2). The description shows 4 peaks totaling 2+2+6+2=12 electrons, placing it in Period 3.

choices.text: 8 valence electrons; the element is neon (Ne)
choices.explanation: Neon has 10 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ and only 3 peaks. The spectrum described has 4 peaks with 12 total electrons.

choices.text: 2 valence electrons; the element is magnesium (Mg)
choices.explanation: Total electrons = 2+2+6+2 = 12 = magnesium (Z=12). The lowest binding energy peak (5 eV, 2 electrons) represents the $3s^2$ valence electrons. The higher-energy peaks represent $1s^2$, $2s^2$, and $2p^6$ core electrons.

choices.text: 6 valence electrons; the element is sulfur (S)
choices.explanation: Sulfur has 16 electrons. The described spectrum has 2+2+6+2=12 electrons total — not 16. The element is magnesium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following sequences correctly ranks the species $Na^+$, $Mg^{2+}$, and $Al^{3+}$ in order of increasing ionic radius?

choices.explanation: This is the opposite of the correct order. All three are isoelectronic (10 electrons each), and higher nuclear charge means smaller ionic radius.

All three ions are isoelectronic (same electron configuration, 10 electrons). More protons in the nucleus with the same electron count means stronger attraction and smaller radius. Al³⁺ (Z=13) < Mg²⁺ (Z=12) < Na⁺ (Z=11).
For isoelectronic species, more protons = smaller radius. Al³⁺ (Z=13) is smallest; Na⁺ (Z=11) is largest. This ordering is incorrect.
This ordering places Mg²⁺ as largest, but Na⁺ should be largest among these isoelectronic ions (fewest protons per electron). Correct order: Al³⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Na⁺.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A chemist has 3.01 × 10²³ atoms of gold (Au, molar mass = 197 g/mol). What is the mass of this sample?

choices.explanation: 197 g corresponds to 1 mole ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms). The sample has $3.01 \times 10^{23}$ atoms = 0.5 mole, which has half the mass.
59.2 g ≈ 197/3.33 — this does not correspond to the correct calculation. 0.5 mol Au × 197 g/mol = 98.5 g.
394 g = 2 × 197 g (2 moles). The sample has only half a mole ($3.01 \times 10^{23}$ atoms), so the mass is 0.5 × 197 = 98.5 g.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The successive ionization energies of an unknown element X are: IE₁ = 590, IE₂ = 1145, IE₃ = 4912, IE₄ = 6491 kJ/mol. In which group of the periodic table is element X most likely found?

choices.explanation: Group 1 elements would show a dramatic jump between IE₁ and IE₂. The data shows IE₁ and IE₂ are relatively similar (590 to 1145 — about doubling), but the large jump is between IE₂ and IE₃.

The large jump occurs between IE₂ and IE₃ (1145 to 4912 kJ/mol — a 4-fold increase). This indicates 2 valence electrons — after removing both valence electrons, the third must come from a core shell, requiring dramatically more energy. Element X is in Group 2.
Group 3 would show the large jump between IE₃ and IE₄. The data shows the large jump occurs between IE₂ and IE₃, indicating only 2 valence electrons.
Group 4 would show the dramatic jump between IE₄ and IE₅. The data shows it between IE₂ and IE₃, indicating 2 valence electrons (Group 2).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Element X has atomic number 35. How many core electrons does element X have?

choices.explanation: 7 is the number of valence electrons in bromine. Core electrons are all electrons NOT in the outermost shell.

Bromine (Z=35) has configuration $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^2 4p^5$. Valence electrons (n=4): $4s^2 4p^5$ = 7. Core electrons = 35 − 7 = 28. Note: $[Ar]$ = 18 electrons + $3d^{10}$ = 10 more = 28 total core electrons.
18 core electrons would be the [Ar] core only, ignoring the $3d^{10}$ electrons that are also core (not in the n=4 valence shell). Total core = 28.
35 is the total number of electrons (atomic number). Core electrons exclude the valence shell (n=4): 35 − 7 valence = 28 core.
